title: The Android Manifest
description: AndroidManifest.xml: component declarations, intent filters, permissions, manifest merging, and advanced features
lesson:
Part 1: Manifest Fundamentals

The AndroidManifest.xml is the contract between your app and the Android system. It is parsed at install time by PackageManager, which extracts everything the system needs to know about your app: its components, required permissions, hardware features, minimum API level, and more. The system never reads your Kotlin/Java source code to discover these things — it relies entirely on the manifest.

When is the manifest read?
1. Install time — PackageManager parses it, stores component info in the package database
2. Intent resolution — AMS queries PackageManager to match intents to components
3. Permission checks — system enforces declared permissions at runtime
4. Play Store filtering — uses-feature and uses-sdk filter device compatibility

Top-Level Structure

// Every AndroidManifest.xml has this structure: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?> <manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android" xmlns:tools="http://schemas.android.com/tools"> <uses-permission .../> <uses-feature .../> <uses-sdk .../> <queries>...</queries> <application android:name=".MyApplication" android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher" android:label="@string/app_name" android:theme="@style/AppTheme"> <activity .../> <service .../> <receiver .../> <provider .../> <meta-data .../> </application> </manifest>

The <manifest> Tag

The root element. The package attribute used to serve double duty: it was both the application ID and the R class namespace. Since AGP 7.0+, the application ID is set in build.gradle via applicationId, and the manifest package attribute is only used as the namespace for the generated R class. In AGP 8.0+, even that moved to the namespace property in build.gradle, so the package attribute is no longer required.

<uses-sdk> — minSdk, targetSdk, compileSdk

These three values control different things and are commonly confused:

PropertySet InWhat It Means
minSdkbuild.gradleLowest API level your app will install on. Play Store uses this for filtering. Code below this API must use compat libraries.
targetSdkbuild.gradleThe API level you have tested against. The system uses this to enable/disable behavior changes. If targetSdk=33, you get API 34 behavior changes only after you bump it.
compileSdkbuild.gradle onlyThe API level the compiler can see. Determines which APIs you can call in source code. Does NOT appear in the manifest. Does NOT affect runtime behavior.
Key insight: compileSdk is compile-time only. targetSdk is a runtime compatibility flag — the system changes behavior based on it. For example, targeting API 31+ requires you to set exported on all components with intent filters. Targeting API 33+ requires runtime permission for notifications (POST_NOTIFICATIONS).

The <application> Tag

Wraps all four component types and sets app-wide defaults:

AttributePurpose
android:nameCustom Application subclass (e.g., .MyApp). Created before any component. Used by Hilt (@HiltAndroidApp), crash reporters, analytics init.
android:iconLauncher icon (usually @mipmap/ic_launcher). Components can override.
android:labelUser-visible app name.
android:themeDefault theme for all activities.
android:allowBackupWhether the system can back up app data. Default true. Set to false for sensitive apps.
android:supportsRtlEnable right-to-left layout mirroring.
android:networkSecurityConfigPoints to XML defining trusted CAs, cleartext traffic rules, certificate pinning.
android:dataExtractionRulesAPI 31+ replacement for fullBackupContent. Controls cloud vs device-to-device backup rules.
android:fullBackupContentPre-API 31 backup rules. Points to XML specifying include/exclude paths.

Part 2: Component Declarations

Every component your app uses must be declared in the manifest (or merged from a library manifest). The system cannot start a component it doesn't know about.

<activity>

<activity android:name=".ui.MainActivity" android:exported="true" android:launchMode="singleTop" android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize" android:screenOrientation="portrait" android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize"> <intent-filter> <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" /> </intent-filter> </activity>

Key attributes:

AttributePurpose
nameFully qualified class name (.ui.MainActivity is shorthand for com.example.app.ui.MainActivity).
exportedWhether other apps can start this activity. Required if intent filters are present (API 31+).
launchModeControls task/back-stack behavior (see below).
taskAffinityWhich task this activity "prefers." Default = app's package name. Used with singleTask.
configChangesConfig changes the activity handles itself (no recreate). Common: orientation|screenSize|uiMode.
screenOrientationLock to portrait/landscape. Avoid unless necessary (bad for tablets/foldables).
windowSoftInputModeHow the window adjusts when the soft keyboard appears: adjustResize, adjustPan, adjustNothing.

Launch Modes

launchMode Behavior Use Case ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── standard New instance every time. Default. Most activities. A → B → B → B (3 B instances) singleTop If already on TOP of stack, Notification targets, reuse via onNewIntent(). search results. A → B → (launch B) → onNewIntent() singleTask Only one instance in the task. Main/home activities. Clears everything above it. A → B → C → (launch A) → A (B,C popped) singleInstance Sole activity in its own task. Call screens, maps. No other activities share its task. singleInstancePerTask Like singleInstance but Multi-window scenarios (API 31+) one per task (allows multiple (different tasks can each tasks to each have one). have one instance).

<service>

<service android:name=".sync.SyncService" android:exported="false" android:foregroundServiceType="dataSync" android:permission="com.example.BIND_SYNC" android:process=":sync" />
AttributePurpose
exportedfalse = only your app can bind/start. true = other apps can too.
foregroundServiceTypeRequired for API 34+ when running foreground services. Values: camera, dataSync, location, mediaPlayback, microphone, etc. Must also declare matching uses-permission.
permissionA permission callers must hold to bind/start this service.
processRun in a separate process. :sync creates a private process named com.example.app:sync.

<receiver>

<receiver android:name=".BootReceiver" android:exported="true" android:enabled="true" android:permission="android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETED"> <intent-filter> <action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED" /> </intent-filter> </receiver>
API 31+ change: If a receiver has intent filters, you must explicitly set exported="true" or exported="false". Before API 31, receivers with intent filters defaulted to exported=true. This was a security risk — apps could inadvertently expose receivers. Now the system enforces explicitness. For dynamically registered receivers (in code), use Context.RECEIVER_EXPORTED or Context.RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED flags.

<provider>

<provider android:name=".data.AppContentProvider" android:authorities="com.example.app.provider" android:exported="false" android:grantUriPermissions="true" android:readPermission="com.example.READ_DATA" android:writePermission="com.example.WRITE_DATA" />
AttributePurpose
authoritiesUnique URI authority (like a domain). Used in content://com.example.app.provider/items.
exportedWhether other apps can query this provider.
grantUriPermissionsAllow temporary per-URI access grants via Intent flags (used by FileProvider for sharing files).
readPermission / writePermissionFine-grained access control. Callers must hold these permissions.

How PackageManager Resolves Components

App Install (APK → PackageManager) │ ├─ Parse AndroidManifest.xml │ ├─ Extract package name, version, SDK requirements │ ├─ Register all 4 component types in package database │ ├─ Index all intent filters for resolution │ └─ Record permissions (requested + defined) │ ├─ Store in /data/system/packages.xml │ └─ At Runtime: │ ├─ startActivity(intent) → AMS → PackageManager.resolveActivity() │ └─ Scans all registered activities' intent filters │ ├─ startService(intent) → AMS → PackageManager.resolveService() │ ├─ sendBroadcast(intent) → AMS → PackageManager.queryBroadcastReceivers() │ └─ Returns ALL matching receivers (broadcasts are 1-to-many) │ └─ getContentResolver().query(uri) → PackageManager.resolveContentProvider() └─ Matches by authority string (exact match, not filters)

Part 3: Intent Filters

Intent filters declare what implicit intents a component can handle. They are the mechanism that makes Android's inter-app communication work — any app can say "I can handle sharing images" and the system will include it in the chooser.

<intent-filter> Structure

<intent-filter> <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" /> <data android:scheme="https" android:host="example.com" android:pathPrefix="/product" /> </intent-filter>

Intent Resolution: Three Tests

For an implicit intent to match a component's filter, it must pass all three tests:

Implicit Intent │ ├─ 1. ACTION TEST │ Intent's action must match at least one <action> in the filter. │ Filter with no actions → nothing matches. │ Intent with no action → matches any filter (rare). │ ├─ 2. CATEGORY TEST │ Every category in the Intent must exist in the filter. │ IMPORTANT: startActivity() auto-adds CATEGORY_DEFAULT! │ So your filter almost always needs: │ <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" /> │ └─ 3. DATA TEST Matches scheme, host, port, path, and/or mimeType. Rules are complex: ├─ Intent has URI only → filter must match URI parts ├─ Intent has MIME only → filter must match MIME ├─ Intent has both → filter must match both └─ Intent has neither → filter must have neither ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ALL THREE must pass. If any test fails, no match.

MAIN + LAUNCHER

// This intent filter makes an activity appear in the launcher <intent-filter> <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" /> </intent-filter> // MAIN = entry point (no data expected) // LAUNCHER = show in launcher grid // Note: this filter does NOT need DEFAULT because // the launcher starts it with an explicit intent

Deep Links, App Links & Custom Schemes

Custom URL SchemeWeb Links (Deep Links)App Links (Verified)
Schememyapp://http:// or https://https:// only
DisambiguationNo chooser (your scheme)User sees chooser dialogOpens directly (no chooser)
VerificationNoneNoneSystem verifies via /.well-known/assetlinks.json on your server
autoVerifyN/ANot setandroid:autoVerify="true"
FallbackError if no handlerOpens in browser if user picks itOpens in browser if verification fails
SecurityAny app can register same schemeAny app can register same URLOnly verified domain owner
// App Link (verified HTTPS deep link) <intent-filter android:autoVerify="true"> <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" /> <data android:scheme="https" android:host="www.example.com" android:pathPrefix="/product" /> </intent-filter> // Server must host: // https://www.example.com/.well-known/assetlinks.json // containing your app's signing certificate fingerprint
Best practice: Prefer App Links (verified HTTPS) over custom URL schemes. Custom schemes like myapp:// have no ownership verification — any app can register the same scheme and intercept your links. App Links are cryptographically verified.

Part 4: Permissions in the Manifest

<uses-permission> — Requesting Permissions

// Request permissions your app needs <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" /> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.CAMERA" /> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION" /> // Conditional permission (only on API 33+) <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS" android:maxSdkVersion="99" /> // Foreground service permissions (API 34+) <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE" /> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_LOCATION" />

Permissions fall into protection levels: normal (auto-granted at install, e.g., INTERNET), dangerous (requires runtime prompt, e.g., CAMERA), and signature (granted only if signed with same cert as the defining app).

<permission> — Defining Custom Permissions

// Define a custom permission in your manifest <permission android:name="com.example.app.permission.READ_SETTINGS" android:label="Read App Settings" android:description="@string/perm_read_settings_desc" android:protectionLevel="signature" /> // Then guard a component with it: <provider android:name=".SettingsProvider" android:authorities="com.example.app.settings" android:readPermission="com.example.app.permission.READ_SETTINGS" /> // protectionLevel values: // normal — auto-granted, no prompt // dangerous — requires runtime permission dialog // signature — only apps signed with same key can use

<uses-feature> — Hardware/Software Requirements

// Required feature: app won't install without camera <uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.camera" android:required="true" /> // Optional feature: app works without GPS but uses it if available <uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.location.gps" android:required="false" />
Google Play filtering: The Play Store reads <uses-feature> to hide your app from incompatible devices. If required="true" (the default!), devices without that feature won't even see your app. Additionally, some permissions imply features — e.g., CAMERA permission implies android.hardware.camera. If your app can work without a camera, you must explicitly declare required="false" to override the implication.

Part 5: Manifest Merging

Your final APK has a single merged manifest. But it's built from multiple sources:

Merge Priority (highest wins): 1. Build variant manifest (e.g., src/debug/AndroidManifest.xml) │ ▼ merges into 2. App module main manifest (src/main/AndroidManifest.xml) │ ▼ merges with 3. Library module manifests (each library's AndroidManifest.xml) │ ▼ merges with 4. AAR dependency manifests (from Maven/local AARs) │ ▼ MERGED MANIFEST (in build/intermediates/merged_manifests/)

Merge Conflict Resolution with tools: Namespace

// A library sets allowBackup="true", you want "false" // Without tools:replace, the merger throws an error <application android:allowBackup="false" tools:replace="android:allowBackup"> // tools:remove — remove an attribute set by a library <application tools:remove="android:supportsRtl"> // tools:node="remove" — remove an entire element from a library <activity android:name="com.library.UnwantedActivity" tools:node="remove" /> // tools:node="replace" — replace library's entire element with yours <activity android:name="com.library.SomeActivity" android:exported="false" tools:node="replace" /> // tools:node="removeAll" — remove ALL elements of that type from libs <uses-permission tools:node="removeAll" />
Common merge conflict: A library declares exported="true" on a receiver, but you want it false. Solution:
<receiver android:name="com.library.SomeReceiver" android:exported="false" tools:node="replace" />

Viewing the merged manifest: In Android Studio, open any AndroidManifest.xml and click the "Merged Manifest" tab at the bottom. It shows the final result with color-coded sources.

Placeholder Injection

// In build.gradle.kts: android { defaultConfig { manifestPlaceholders["appAuthRedirectScheme"] = "com.example.app" manifestPlaceholders["mapsApiKey"] = localProperties["MAPS_KEY"] ?: "" } } // In AndroidManifest.xml: <activity android:name=".auth.RedirectActivity"> <intent-filter> <data android:scheme="${appAuthRedirectScheme}" /> </intent-filter> </activity> <meta-data android:name="com.google.android.geo.API_KEY" android:value="${mapsApiKey}" /> // ${applicationId} is always available — replaced with the app's applicationId <provider android:name="androidx.core.content.FileProvider" android:authorities="${applicationId}.fileprovider" />

Part 6: Advanced Manifest Features

<queries> — Package Visibility (API 30+)

Starting with API 30, apps can no longer see all installed apps by default. You must declare which apps or intent patterns you need to interact with:

// Declare specific packages you need to query <queries> <!-- Specific app --> <package android:name="com.whatsapp" /> <!-- Any app that handles this intent --> <intent> <action android:name="android.intent.action.SEND" /> <data android:mimeType="image/*" /> </intent> <!-- Any app with a content provider at this authority --> <provider android:authorities="com.example.other.provider" /> </queries> // Without <queries>, packageManager.getInstalledPackages() // returns empty or filtered results on API 30+

<meta-data> — Library Configuration

// Pass configuration to libraries via meta-data // Firebase Cloud Messaging: default notification channel <meta-data android:name="com.google.firebase.messaging.default_notification_channel_id" android:value="@string/default_channel_id" /> // Google Maps API key <meta-data android:name="com.google.android.geo.API_KEY" android:value="${mapsApiKey}" /> // Disable auto-init for Firebase Analytics <meta-data android:name="firebase_analytics_collection_enabled" android:value="false" /> // Libraries read these at runtime via: // appInfo.metaData.getString("com.google.android.geo.API_KEY")

Process Attributes

By default, all components run in the app's main process. You can isolate components into separate processes:

<service android:name=".WebViewService" android:process=":webview" /> // ":webview" = private process (com.example.app:webview) // "com.example.shared" = global process (other apps can share it) // Each process gets its own Application instance! // Your Application.onCreate() runs once PER PROCESS. // Static state is NOT shared across processes. // Use IPC (Binder/AIDL, ContentProvider) to communicate.
Multi-process pitfall: If you run a Service in :sync process and your Application.onCreate() initializes heavy libraries (Firebase, Hilt), those libraries initialize again in the second process, wasting memory and time. Guard init code with a process name check.

Multi-Module and Dynamic Feature Manifests

In a multi-module project, each module has its own AndroidManifest.xml:

:app module └── AndroidManifest.xml (main manifest, highest priority) :feature:auth module └── AndroidManifest.xml └── Declares LoginActivity, AuthService :feature:payments module └── AndroidManifest.xml └── Declares PaymentActivity, BillingProvider :core:network module (library module) └── AndroidManifest.xml └── Declares INTERNET permission, NetworkSecurityConfig ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ All merged at build time into one manifest. Library permissions propagate up to :app. Components in feature modules are included in the APK.

For Dynamic Feature modules (delivered on demand via Play Feature Delivery), components are declared in the feature's manifest and installed at runtime via SplitInstallManager. The system discovers them only after the feature is installed. Activities in dynamic features must be launched via an explicit intent using their fully qualified class name.

questions.title: minSdk vs targetSdk vs compileSdk
questions.description: Your app has minSdk = 26, targetSdk = 33, compileSdk = 34. Which statement is true?
options: The app can use API 34 features at runtime without checks
The app won't install on devices running API 25 or below, the compiler can see API 34 APIs, but runtime behavior changes for API 34 are NOT applied since targetSdk is 33
compileSdk 34 means the app runs API 34 behavior on all devices
targetSdk 33 means the app cannot install on API 34 devices
questions.explanation: minSdk is the install floor — API 25 and below are blocked. compileSdk controls which APIs the compiler can see, so you can reference API 34 classes (with runtime version checks). targetSdk is a runtime compatibility flag — the system only enables behavior changes for API levels up to targetSdk. Since targetSdk is 33, API 34 behavior changes (like further background restrictions) are NOT enforced yet. The app still installs and runs on API 34 devices — it just gets legacy behavior for 34-specific changes.
hints: compileSdk is compile-time only, it doesn't ship in the APK
targetSdk tells the system which behavior changes to apply

questions.title: Exported Requirement (API 31+)
questions.description: You add an intent filter to a <receiver> in your manifest but forget to set exported. What happens on a device running API 31+?
options: The receiver defaults to exported="true" and works fine
The receiver defaults to exported="false" and silently ignores external broadcasts
The app fails to install with an error about the missing exported attribute
The app installs but crashes at runtime when a broadcast is received
questions.explanation: Starting with API 31 (Android 12), any component with an <intent-filter> must explicitly declare android:exported. If you omit it, the app fails to install — the installer (PackageManager) rejects the APK. This was a security change to prevent accidentally exporting components. Before API 31, components with intent filters defaulted to exported=true, which was a common source of security vulnerabilities.
hints: API 31 made this a hard requirement, not just a warning
Think about what PackageManager does at install time

questions.title: Intent Filter Matching
questions.description: An activity has this intent filter. Which of the following intents will match?
questions.code:
<intent-filter>
    <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" />
    <data
        android:scheme="https"
        android:host="example.com"
        android:pathPrefix="/products" />
</intent-filter>

// Intent A: action=VIEW, data=https://example.com/products/123
// Intent B: action=VIEW, data=https://example.com/about
// Intent C: action=SEND, data=https://example.com/products/456
// Intent D: action=VIEW, data=http://example.com/products/789
options: All four intents match
Only Intent A matches — correct action, scheme, host, and pathPrefix
Intents A and D match — both have action VIEW and the right host/path
Intents A and B match — both have action VIEW and correct scheme/host
questions.explanation:
Intent resolution requires ALL three tests to pass:
Intent A: Action=VIEW (matches), categories include DEFAULT (auto-added by startActivity) and BROWSABLE (matches), data scheme=https + host=example.com + path starts with /products (matches). PASSES.
Intent B: Path is /about, not /products*. Data test fails.
Intent C: Action is SEND, not VIEW. Action test fails.
Intent D: Scheme is http, filter requires https. Data test fails.
hints: All three tests (action, category, data) must pass
pathPrefix="/products" matches /products, /products/123, /products/abc — but not /about

questions.title: Launch Mode Behavior
questions.description: Activity A starts Activity B (launchMode="singleTask", taskAffinity=default). Then B starts C, then C starts D. Now D starts B again. What is the back stack?
options: A → B → C → D → B (new instance of B on top)
A → B (C and D are popped, B receives onNewIntent)
A → B → C → D (B is brought to front, D stays)
B only (everything else is destroyed)
questions.explanation: With singleTask, the system looks for an existing instance of B in its preferred task. It finds B already in the stack. Rather than creating a new instance, it clears everything above B (C and D are destroyed) and delivers the intent to B via onNewIntent(). The final stack is A → B. This is why singleTask is used for main/home screens — returning to them always clears the stack above.
hints: singleTask means only one instance in the task
What happens to activities above the existing instance?

questions.title: Manifest Merging Conflict
questions.description: A library dependency sets android:allowBackup="true" in its manifest. Your app sets android:allowBackup="false". What happens during the build?
options: Your app's value wins automatically since it has higher priority
The library's value wins since it was added first
The build fails with a manifest merge error — you must use tools:replace to resolve it
Both values are kept and the system picks one at runtime
questions.explanation:
When the same attribute is set to different values in manifests at different priority levels, the manifest merger reports a conflict error and the build fails. Unlike some merge rules, attribute conflicts are not silently resolved. You must explicitly tell the merger what to do:
<application android:allowBackup="false" tools:replace="android:allowBackup">
This tells the merger: "I know the library sets this differently. Use my value."
hints: The merger is conservative — it won't silently override library values
The tools: namespace provides merge directives

questions.title: Deep Links vs App Links
questions.description: What is the key difference between a regular deep link and an App Link?
options: Deep links use HTTP, App Links use HTTPS
App Links require android:autoVerify="true" and server-side assetlinks.json verification, which allows the system to open your app directly without a disambiguation dialog
Deep links work only on API 23+, App Links work on all versions
App Links require a custom URL scheme like myapp://
questions.explanation: App Links are verified HTTPS deep links. When you set android:autoVerify="true", the system fetches /.well-known/assetlinks.json from your domain at install time and checks that it contains your app's signing certificate. If verified, your app opens directly when users click matching URLs — no disambiguation dialog. Regular deep links (without verification) show a chooser if multiple apps can handle the URL. App Links give you guaranteed ownership of your URLs.
hints: Think about what prevents another app from claiming your URLs
The key word is 'verified'

questions.title: uses-feature Filtering Problem
questions.description: This app optionally uses the camera for scanning QR codes, but many users report the app is not visible on the Play Store for tablets without cameras. Find and fix the issue.

checks.message: Add a <uses-feature> declaration for the camera
Set required="false" since the camera is optional

questions.explanation: The CAMERA permission implicitly adds <uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.camera" android:required="true">. This tells the Play Store to hide the app from devices without cameras. Since the camera is optional (QR scanning is a bonus feature), you must explicitly declare <uses-feature> with required="false" to override the implied requirement. Always check implied features when adding hardware-related permissions.
hints: Some permissions imply hardware features
What does the Play Store assume when it sees the CAMERA permission?

questions.title: Package Visibility (API 30+)
questions.description: Explain what <queries> does in the manifest and why it was introduced in API 30.
questions.answer: The <queries> element declares which other apps your app needs to interact with. Introduced in API 30 for privacy: before API 30, any app could enumerate all installed apps via PackageManager, which was a fingerprinting/privacy risk. Now apps can only see a filtered set. You declare visibility needs via: specific package names, intent filter patterns, or provider authorities. Without proper <queries> declarations, calls like resolveActivity(), getInstalledPackages(), and queryIntentActivities() return empty/filtered results.
questions.explanation: Package visibility was a major privacy improvement. Apps like shopping apps were scanning installed apps to build user profiles. By requiring explicit <queries> declarations, Android limits each app's view to only the packages it legitimately needs to interact with. The QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES permission exists as an escape hatch but is heavily restricted on the Play Store.
hints: Think about why an app might want to see all installed apps — and why that's a privacy concern
What happens to PackageManager queries without proper declarations?

questions.title: Foreground Service Type (API 34+)
questions.description: This app targets API 34. What happens when it tries to start the foreground service?
questions.code:
<!-- In AndroidManifest.xml -->
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE" />

<service
    android:name=".LocationTrackingService"
    android:exported="false" />

// In Kotlin code:
val intent = Intent(this, LocationTrackingService::class.java)
startForegroundService(intent)

// Inside LocationTrackingService:
override fun onStartCommand(...): Int {
    val notification = buildNotification()
    startForeground(1, notification, FOREGROUND_SERVICE_TYPE_LOCATION)
    return START_STICKY
}
options: The service starts normally with a notification
The service crashes with MissingForegroundServiceTypeException because the manifest does not declare foregroundServiceType
The service starts but the notification is not shown
The app fails to compile because FOREGROUND_SERVICE_TYPE_LOCATION is not a valid constant
questions.explanation: On API 34+, foreground services must declare their type in the manifest via android:foregroundServiceType. Additionally, you need the type-specific permission (FOREGROUND_SERVICE_LOCATION). This manifest is missing both: no foregroundServiceType="location" on the <service> tag, and no FOREGROUND_SERVICE_LOCATION permission. The call to startForeground() with a type that wasn't declared in the manifest throws MissingForegroundServiceTypeException.
hints: API 34 added strict requirements for foreground service types
Check what's missing from the <service> declaration

questions.title: Manifest Merge Resolution
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to override a library's manifest settings. The library sets exported="true" on its activity, but you want it false. The library also adds an unwanted permission.
questions.code:
<manifest
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    xmlns:tools="http://schemas.android.com/tools">

    <!-- Remove the unwanted permission from library -->
    <uses-permission
        android:name="com.library.UNWANTED_PERMISSION"
        ___1___ />

    <application ...>
        <!-- Override library's activity to be not exported -->
        <activity
            android:name="com.library.SomeActivity"
            android:exported="false"
            ___2___ />
    </application>
</manifest>

blanks.hint: Which tools: directive completely removes an element from the merge?
Which tools: directive replaces the library's element/attribute with yours?

questions.explanation: tools:node="remove" tells the manifest merger to completely remove that element (the unwanted permission) from the merged result. For the activity, you can use either tools:node="replace" (replaces the entire element) or tools:replace="android:exported" (replaces just that attribute). Both work, but tools:replace is more surgical — it keeps the library's other attributes intact while only overriding the ones you specify.
hints: The tools: namespace provides merge directives
One directive removes elements, another replaces them
